matches.snippet: as  high  as  a  howeth,  the  famous  eld  duke  alien,  with  a  hump
matches.reasoning: Howth (Dublin landmark = Finn's head in the giant-as-landscape motif). 'Howeth' is a garbled spelling that evokes this.

matches.snippet: Well,  old  Humber  was  as  glommen  as  grampus,  with  the  tares  at  his  thor
matches.reasoning: Humber (river in England) as geographical body; part of the recumbent giant landscape motif associated with Finn/HCE. 'Old Humber' echoes 'old Finnegan.'

matches.snippet: bales  allbrant  on  the  crests  of  rockies  and  nera  lamp  in  kitchen  or church  and  giant's  holes  in  Grafton's  causeway
matches.reasoning: Direct reference to 'giant's holes'—part of the physical Dublin terrain as Finn's body, his traces left in the landscape.

matches.snippet: sittang  sambre  on  his  sett,  drammen  and  drommen,  usking  queasy
matches.reasoning: Sett = seat or dwelling; part of the sleeping giant HCE/Finn as landscape feature, sitting on his 'sett' in the Dublin terrain.

matches.snippet: Don't  you  know  he  was  kaldt  a  bairn  of  the  brine,  Wasserbourne  the  waterbaby?  Havemmarea,  so  he  was!  H.C.E.  has  a  codfisck  ee.
matches.reasoning: HCE is explicitly Joyce's main avatar for Finn MacCool/Finnegan. This is the direct statement that HCE (Here Comes Everybody / the sleeping giant) has certain characteristics.
